content.text: When an AI agent makes a mistake in production, the most direct correction is human feedback: a reviewer indicates what the agent should have done instead. Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) formalizes this intuition into a training pipeline that systematically incorporates human preferences into the agent's behavior — not just as prompt instructions, but as learned weights that shape how the model reasons at inference time.

RLHF is a three-phase training procedure that uses human judgment to shape the behavior of a language model beyond what supervised fine-tuning on demonstrations alone can achieve. The three phases are: (1)
on human demonstrations to give the model a starting point, (2)
on human preference comparisons to learn a scalar signal that captures human judgment, and (3)
to optimize the model's policy against that learned reward signal while maintaining proximity to the SFT baseline.
In the context of production agent systems, RLHF addresses a fundamental limitation of prompt engineering and supervised fine-tuning: both require the engineer to specify what

looks like in advance, either in language (prompt) or in labeled examples (SFT). Human preferences, by contrast, are often easier to express comparatively — "response A is better than response B" — and RLHF builds on this comparative judgment to learn a reward model that can generalize to novel situations.

Prompt engineering and SFT on demonstrations plateau. They work well when the desired behavior can be exhaustively described or demonstrated, but production agents encounter inputs that no engineer anticipated. A model trained only on demonstrations generalizes from those demonstrations by pattern-matching; a model trained with RLHF generalizes from
, which is a more robust form of generalization for tasks where quality is judged subjectively.

For production agent systems specifically, RLHF enables improvement in dimensions that are hard to specify explicitly: response tone that users find trustworthy, the right level of detail for a given user's apparent expertise, knowing when to hedge versus when to assert with confidence. These are preferences that humans recognize when they see them but struggle to articulate as rules.
Comparative judgments ("A is better than B") are easier to elicit reliably than absolute quality ratings or demonstrations of ideal behavior.
A trained reward model captures a latent notion of quality that applies to novel inputs, not just the specific comparisons it was trained on.
Behaviors that are hard to specify in a prompt — appropriate assertiveness, calibrated uncertainty, stylistic consistency — can be learned from preferences.
When preference comparisons include safety-relevant judgments (which response is safer?), RLHF can learn safety behaviors alongside quality behaviors.

Collecting preference pairs at the scale needed for a reliable reward model (thousands to tens of thousands of comparisons) is expensive. Managed annotation services charge $1–5 per comparison for complex agent outputs.

Once you optimize a model against a learned reward signal rather than the true human preference, the model may learn to score highly on the reward model through behaviors that humans would not actually prefer. The reward model is a proxy; optimizing it too hard breaks the proxy.
A full RLHF pipeline — SFT, reward model training, PPO optimization — requires significant ML infrastructure and expertise to run reliably in production.
Annotators do not always agree on which response is better. Measuring and managing inter-annotator agreement is a prerequisite for high-quality preference data.
Before RLHF can begin, the base model must be aligned to the target task via SFT on human demonstrations. The SFT step produces a starting policy

that already behaves approximately correctly. RLHF then refines π₀ rather than training from scratch. Skipping SFT and applying RLHF directly to a base model produces poor results because the base model's initial behavior is too diverse for preference comparisons to be meaningful.
Human annotators are shown pairs of responses (A, B) to the same prompt and asked to indicate which they prefer. The reward model is trained on these comparisons using the
, which models the probability that response A is preferred over response B as:
is the scalar reward assigned to response
by the reward model. Training minimizes the negative log-likelihood of the observed human preferences:

The SFT policy π₀ is fine-tuned using RL to maximize the learned reward signal, subject to a KL-divergence penalty that prevents the policy from drifting too far from π₀ (which would cause incoherent text generation):
controls the trade-off between reward maximization and staying close to the SFT baseline. Higher
= more aggressive optimization (higher reward hacking risk).

attrs.alt: RLHF pipeline for production agents: Phase 1 SFT on demonstrations produces policy π₀. Phase 2 Preference Data collection from comparison UI feeds Bradley-Terry reward model training. Phase 3 RL Policy Optimization via PPO or DPO produces π_RLHF. Deployment with A/B testing and reward hacking monitoring feeds back into preference collection. Constitutional AI is shown as a complementary technique.

content.text: The pipeline is a directed cycle. The
runs once to produce the initial policy.
run periodically as new comparisons accumulate.
runs each time the reward model is updated.
is gated by A/B testing, and production observations feed back into new preference data collection.
If quality cannot be reduced to a deterministic rule (correct/incorrect), RLHF's comparative preference framework is better suited than SFT on labeled demonstrations.
When you have annotator budget and infrastructure.
A reward model trained on fewer than 1,000 preference pairs is unreliable. Budget for the annotation and the ML infrastructure before committing to RLHF.
If multiple rounds of prompt iteration have not resolved a quality gap, RLHF can address the gap at the model weight level rather than the prompt level.
As a complement to Constitutional AI.
RLHF from user preference data shapes task quality; Constitutional AI shapes safety and refusal behavior. Both are used together in mature production systems.

# Preference data collection schema for production agent outputs.
# Supports both pairwise comparison (A vs B) and direct rating signals.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import datetime
from typing import Literal, Optional
import uuid

@dataclass
class PreferenceRecord:
    record_id: str = field(default_factory=lambda: str(uuid.uuid4()))
    created_at: datetime = field(default_factory=datetime.utcnow)

    # ── Task context ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    prompt: str = ""           # the prompt shown to the model
    response_a: str = ""       # one candidate response
    response_b: str = ""       # the other candidate response
    model_version_a: str = ""  # which model/prompt version produced A
    model_version_b: str = ""  # which model/prompt version produced B

    # ── Human judgment ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
    preferred: Literal["A", "B", "tie"] = "A"
    # For annotator confidence / inter-annotator agreement:
    confidence: Literal["confident", "unsure"] = "confident"
    annotator_id: str = ""
    annotation_time_sec: Optional[int] = None  # how long the annotator spent

    # ── Dimension scores (optional, for analysis) ─────────────────────────
    # If the annotation UI collects per-dimension ratings in addition to
    # the overall preference, record them here.
    dimension_scores: dict = field(default_factory=dict)
    # e.g. {"accuracy": {"A": 4, "B": 2}, "helpfulness": {"A": 3, "B": 4}}

    # ── Filtering flags ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
    contains_pii: bool = False      # flagged for scrubbing before training
    safety_flagged: bool = False    # flagged for safety review
    in_training_set: bool = False   # included in reward model training data

# Evaluating a reward model's correlation with human judgment.
# Key risk: the model may have memorized the training distribution.
# Always evaluate on a held-out test set of preference comparisons.

import numpy as np
from scipy import stats
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score

def evaluate_reward_model(
    reward_model,
    test_records: list[PreferenceRecord],
) -> dict:
    """
    Evaluate reward model quality on held-out preference comparisons.

    Returns:
      - preference_accuracy: fraction of test pairs where the RM
        correctly predicts the human preference
      - rank_correlation: Spearman correlation between RM scores
        and human preference strength (confidence-weighted)
    """
    correct = 0
    rm_margins = []    # r(A) - r(B) as predicted by the reward model
    human_margins = [] # +1 if human prefers A, -1 if human prefers B

    for rec in test_records:
        if rec.preferred == "tie":
            continue  # exclude ties from accuracy evaluation

        score_a = reward_model.score(rec.prompt, rec.response_a)
        score_b = reward_model.score(rec.prompt, rec.response_b)
        rm_margins.append(score_a - score_b)

        human_margin = 1 if rec.preferred == "A" else -1
        # Weight by annotator confidence: confident=1.0, unsure=0.5
        weight = 1.0 if rec.confidence == "confident" else 0.5
        human_margins.append(human_margin * weight)

        predicted = "A" if score_a > score_b else "B"
        if predicted == rec.preferred:
            correct += 1

    n = len([r for r in test_records if r.preferred != "tie"])
    accuracy = correct / n if n > 0 else 0.0
    rank_corr, p_value = stats.spearmanr(rm_margins, human_margins)

    return {
        "preference_accuracy": accuracy,
        "rank_correlation": rank_corr,
        "p_value": p_value,
        "n_test_pairs": n,
    }
DPO as a Practical Alternative to PPO

# Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) converts RLHF into a supervised learning
# objective, avoiding the complexity of a separate reward model and PPO training.
# DPO trains the policy directly from preference pairs.
#
# DPO loss for a single preference pair (prompt, chosen, rejected):
#   L_DPO = -log σ( β * log[π(chosen|prompt)/π_ref(chosen|prompt)]
#                 - β * log[π(rejected|prompt)/π_ref(rejected|prompt)] )
#
# This example shows the data format expected by HuggingFace's TRL DPO trainer.

# Training record format for DPO (as expected by trl.DPOTrainer)
dpo_training_record = {
    "prompt": "Summarize this customer complaint in one sentence: ...",
    "chosen": "The customer reports a billing error that resulted in ...",  # preferred
    "rejected": "Customer is unhappy about billing.",                       # not preferred
}

# Advantages of DPO over PPO for production systems:
# 1. No separate reward model to train and maintain.
# 2. Stable training — supervised cross-entropy objective, no RL instability.
# 3. Lower memory footprint — only the policy and reference policy in memory.
# 4. Easier to evaluate — loss function corresponds directly to preference accuracy.
#
# When PPO is still preferred:
# - When the reward signal comes from a verifier (math, code execution)
#   rather than human preferences — PPO handles dynamic reward signals better.
# - When the reward model is already built and high-quality.
# - When RLHF is used iteratively with online data collection.
A preference dataset used for reward model training must satisfy three properties:

Preference pairs should cover the full range of task types the agent handles. A reward model trained primarily on one category of task (e.g., factual questions) will not generalize well to others (e.g., creative writing, code review).

Preference pairs should include ambiguous cases — inputs where the model's current behavior is inconsistent — not just easy ones where the better response is obvious. Easy pairs add less information to the reward model.

For each pair, compute the fraction of annotators who agreed on the preferred response (if using multiple annotators per pair). Pairs with agreement below 60% should be excluded from training; they introduce noise rather than signal.

The minimum viable dataset for a reliable reward model is approximately 1,000–3,000 high-quality preference pairs, covering at least 10 distinct task categories with at least 80 inter-annotator agreement on each category. Below this threshold, the reward model's generalization is unreliable.

A legal research agent summarizes case law for attorneys. After launch, attorneys report that summaries are "too neutral" — they omit the court's tone and the strength of its reasoning, which are important signals for assessing precedential value. This is a tacit preference that is very difficult to encode in a prompt instruction.

The team runs a preference data collection sprint: two summaries of each of 400 cases (one from the production model, one from a model prompted differently) are shown to 8 attorneys. Attorneys choose the better summary and rate their confidence. Inter-annotator agreement averages 74%. They train a reward model on 1,800 preference pairs (400 cases × ~4.5 valid pair comparisons after filtering ties and low-agreement pairs). The reward model achieves 79% preference accuracy on a 200-pair held-out test set.

They run DPO training for 2 epochs with β=0.1. The fine-tuned model is A/B tested at 20% traffic. After 2 weeks, attorney ratings on the A/B test show 68% prefer the RLHF-trained model vs. 22% prefer the control (10% no preference). They deploy the model to 100% of traffic. A post-deployment check at 30 days shows no regression in factual accuracy (measured by a separate hallucination evaluation) and no shift in safety metrics.
Constitutional AI as Complement to RLHF

Constitutional AI (CAI), developed by Anthropic, is a technique where a model critiques and revises its own outputs against a set of written principles (a "constitution") without requiring human feedback on the content of the revision. The revised outputs are then used as supervised training data, and separately, preference data is generated synthetically by having the model compare its own outputs.
CAI and RLHF address different aspects of behavior. RLHF from user preferences shapes
— helpfulness, accuracy, tone, completeness. CAI shapes

— which requests the model should decline, how to handle sensitive topics, how to respond to adversarial inputs. Production systems at Anthropic use both: CAI during model training to establish safety properties, and RLHF from human feedback to refine quality properties. For teams building on top of foundation models, the equivalent is using system prompts and prompt engineering for CAI-like safety constraints, while RLHF handles quality improvement.

Goodhart's Law states: "When a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure." In RLHF, the reward model is the measure; the optimized policy is the target. As RL training proceeds, the policy learns to maximize reward model scores — which may diverge from true human preference if the reward model has systematic biases or blind spots.
Signs of reward hacking in production:
If the reward model slightly prefers longer responses, the RL-optimized policy will gradually produce responses that are longer than humans actually want. Monitor average response length as a proxy.

The model agrees with the user's stated position regardless of accuracy, because human annotators tend to prefer responses that validate their views. Monitor factual accuracy on a fixed evaluation set.

The model learns formatting patterns (bullet points, bold headers) that score well on the reward model regardless of whether the content warrants them. Evaluate on tasks where plain prose is clearly appropriate.
Track the KL divergence between the RL-trained policy and the SFT baseline during and after training. A KL that grows well beyond the expected range indicates reward hacking.
hiring a coach rather than writing a rulebook

. A rulebook specifies behavior in advance and is limited by the rules author's ability to anticipate every situation. A coach observes performance and provides comparative feedback ("your approach in that situation was better than the previous one"). Over many rounds of coaching, the performer internalizes a sense of quality that generalizes to situations neither the rulebook nor the coach ever explicitly addressed.

The reward model is the distilled coaching judgment — a scalar function that captures, imperfectly, what makes a response better or worse. The RL optimization is the performer practicing against that coaching feedback. The KL penalty is the constraint that keeps the performer from abandoning everything they learned in the SFT phase while chasing the coach's approval. And monitoring for reward hacking is checking that the performer is genuinely improving, not gaming the coach's evaluation criteria.
Too Few Preference Pairs Before Training the Reward Model

A reward model trained on 200 preference pairs will appear to work on its training distribution but will generalize poorly. The reward model's accuracy on the held-out test set is the leading indicator: an accuracy below 65% on a balanced test set is too low to be useful. Collect more data before proceeding to RL optimization.

Setting β (the KL penalty coefficient) too low allows the RL policy to deviate substantially from the SFT baseline. This reliably produces reward hacking: the model finds high-scoring behaviors that are not actually what humans prefer. Start with β ≥ 0.1 and monitor response quality manually after the first training run before reducing it.
Conflating Helpfulness and Sycophancy in Preference Labels

Human annotators tend to prefer responses that agree with them, validate their views, and tell them what they want to hear. A reward model trained on unchecked preference data will optimize for sycophancy. Annotation guidelines must explicitly instruct annotators to prefer accurate responses over agreeable ones, and to flag cases where they suspect a preference was driven by agreement rather than quality.
Use multiple annotators per preference pair.

At least 3 annotators per pair, with majority vote as the label and inter-annotator agreement as a quality filter. Low-agreement pairs are more often ambiguous or context-dependent than indicative of genuine preference.
Evaluate the reward model before RL training.
If the reward model's test accuracy is below 65%, RL training on it will not improve model quality. Fix the reward model first.
Prefer DPO over PPO for initial RLHF deployments.
DPO is simpler to implement and debug, avoids PPO's training instability, and often achieves comparable quality improvements with fewer engineering resources.
Run A/B tests after every RLHF training iteration.

Never deploy an RLHF-trained model directly to 100% of traffic. A/B test at 10–20% traffic, monitor reward hacking indicators, and only widen the rollout if the quality improvement holds and no regression is detected.
Include adversarial pairs in preference data.

Pairs where one response is slightly better but the difference is subtle (not just obviously good vs. obviously bad) train a more discriminating reward model. Obvious pairs are cheap to label but train a reward model that cannot distinguish subtle quality differences.
Monitor the SFT baseline as a rollback target.
If an RLHF-trained model is rolled back due to reward hacking, the SFT baseline must be available to revert to. Never let the SFT policy expire from model storage.

During RL training (PPO), the reward model is called for every generated response — potentially millions of times. Use a smaller, distilled reward model for online RL training and reserve the full reward model for offline evaluation.

DPO requires both the policy and the reference policy (π_ref) in memory simultaneously, effectively doubling the GPU memory required. Use 4-bit quantization for the reference policy to reduce the footprint.

At 5 minutes per preference pair (including reading and comparing two agent responses), a single annotator produces ~90 pairs per day. To build a 3,000-pair dataset in one week requires 4–5 annotators working full-time on annotations.
The primary open-source library for RLHF and DPO training. Provides
Open-source data annotation platform with built-in support for preference ranking tasks and inter-annotator agreement calculation. Integrates with HuggingFace datasets.
Managed RLHF annotation service. Provides annotators trained on preference comparison tasks, annotation quality checks, and dataset delivery in standard formats.

Training run tracking for RLHF experiments. Logging KL divergence, reward model scores, and preference accuracy across training epochs provides the observability needed to detect reward hacking early.
A distributed RLHF training framework built on Ray, designed for training larger models with PPO. Better scaling than TRL for models above 7B parameters.

Preference pairs are built from production agent outputs, which may contain user data. Scrub PII from both the prompt and the response fields before storing preference records or sending to annotation services.

Users who discover that their feedback influences model training could provide biased feedback to steer the model toward preferred behaviors. Do not expose the feedback-to-training connection publicly, and audit for patterns of systematically biased annotation from single users or groups.
Before deploying an RLHF-trained model, ensure the SFT baseline is tagged and retrievable from model storage. Rollback from an RLHF model to an SFT model must be achievable within minutes, not hours.

Every RLHF training iteration must be evaluated against a safety benchmark before deployment — refusal rates on adversarial inputs, hallucination rates on factual queries, and harmful content rates. RLHF optimizing for helpfulness can inadvertently degrade safety behavior if the preference data did not include safety-relevant comparisons.

RLHF is a three-phase process: SFT on demonstrations produces a baseline policy, reward model training on human preference comparisons learns a quality signal, and RL optimization (PPO or DPO) moves the policy toward higher-reward behavior while staying close to the SFT baseline.
The Bradley-Terry model formalizes pairwise preferences into a scalar reward: P(A ≻ B) = σ(r(A) − r(B)), and reward model training minimizes the negative log-likelihood of observed human preferences.

DPO eliminates the need for a separate reward model and PPO training by converting RLHF into a supervised objective directly on preference pairs, making it significantly more practical for production teams.

Goodhart's Law is the central risk: optimizing against a proxy reward model too aggressively produces reward hacking — the model learns to score well on the reward model through behaviors humans do not actually prefer. The KL penalty coefficient β controls this risk; monitor response length drift, sycophancy, and format exploitation as early warning signals.

Preference dataset quality gates — inter-annotator agreement above 60%, exclusion of sycophancy-driven preferences, diversity across task categories — determine whether the reward model generalizes or memorizes.
RLHF addresses task quality preferences; Constitutional AI addresses safety and refusal behavior. Production systems use both in complementary roles, not as alternatives.

Every RLHF-trained model must be A/B tested before deployment and evaluated against safety benchmarks; the SFT baseline must be retained as a rollback target throughout the RLHF model's production lifetime.
